content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA containing NNN integers.

Find the **smallest** integer xxx that doesn't occur as the LCM of some subsequence of AAA.

The LCM (lowest common multiple) of a set of integers is the smallest positive integer that is a multiple of them all.

For example, LCM(2,3)=6,LCM(4,5,8,1)=40,LCM(13)=13\text{LCM}(2, 3) = 6, \text{LCM}(4, 5, 8, 1) = 40, \text{LCM}(13) = 13LCM(2,3)=6,LCM(4,5,8,1)=40,LCM(13)=13.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of elements of the array.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the smallest integer that cannot be obtained as the LCM of any subsequence of AAA.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 10^5.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the size of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the smallest positive integer that cannot be obtained as the LCM of any non‑empty subsequence of the given array.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Everything from 111 to 555 already appears in AAA, and we obtain 6=LCM(2,3)6 = \text{LCM}(2, 3)6=LCM(2,3).
777 cannot be obtained as the LCM of any subsequence, hence it is the answer.
Test case 222: 111 doesn't appear as the LCM of any subsequence of [2,3,4][2, 3, 4][2,3,4].
Test case 333: 1,2,31, 2, 31,2,3 all appear in the array, while 444 isn't the LCM of any subsequence.

solution.approach:
For each test case we build the set of all LCM values that can be obtained from subsequences.  The classic subset‑DP works because the number of distinct LCMs stays small – each time we add a new array element we combine it with the already known LCMs, compute lcm(old, a) and insert the result.  Duplicates are removed using a hash set.  After processing the whole array we simply scan integers starting from 1 until we find one that is not present in the set; this is the answer.

Why it is fast: The LCM grows quickly, therefore the set never exceeds a few thousand elements even for N = 10^5.  The total work per test case is O(N · |S|) where |S| is the final size of the set (typically ≤ 2000).  This easily fits the limits.

Implementation details:
1. Use 64‑bit integers (long long) – the LCM of numbers ≤ 10^9 fits into 64 bits.
2. lcm(x, y) = x / gcd(x, y) * y to avoid overflow.
3. After each element we create a temporary vector of new values, merge it with the current vector and deduplicate via an unordered_set.
4. Finally, iterate i = 1,2,… and output the first i not found in the set.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N · |S|) per test case, where |S| is the number of distinct LCMs (empirically ≤ a few thousand).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(|S|) for the hash set of LCMs.
